skill.name: grill-with-docs
skill.description: Grilling session that challenges your plan against the existing domain model, sharpens terminology, and updates documentation (CONTEXT.md, ADRs) inline as decisions crystallise. Use when user wants to stress-test a plan against their project's language and documented decisions.
skill.summary: Grilling session that stress-tests plans against domain models and updates documentation inline
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: All static findings are false positives. This is a documentation-only skill containing markdown templates for domain-driven design sessions. No executable code, shell commands, network calls, or file operations beyond documentation updates. The backtick detections are markdown code block formatting, not shell execution. No malicious patterns detected.

content.value_statement: Planning discussions drift into ambiguity. This skill runs structured grilling sessions that force clarity on domain terminology, then updates CONTEXT.md and ADRs inline as understanding crystallizes.
actual_capabilities: Conducting iterative questioning sessions that stress-test design decisions
Detecting terminology conflicts between user language and existing CONTEXT.md glossary
Cross-referencing stated behavior against actual codebase implementation
Updating CONTEXT.md with resolved terminology in real-time
Creating Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) with proper format when warranted
Identifying which decisions are hard to reverse, surprising, or involve real trade-offs
limitations: Does not execute code or run tests to validate decisions
Cannot automatically discover all domain concepts without user input
Relies on user participation for accurate terminology resolution

use_cases.title: Pre-sprint planning clarification
use_cases.description: Before starting a sprint, use this skill to grill the planned features against existing domain language. Catch terminology confusion before it enters code.

use_cases.title: Architecture review preparation
use_cases.description: When evaluating a major architectural decision, use this skill to stress-test assumptions and create a traceable ADR documenting why the decision was made.

use_cases.title: Onboarding documentation gap detection
use_cases.description: New team members use this skill to identify where existing documentation conflicts with actual implementation, surfacing undocumented decisions.
use_cases.target_user: New team member or technical writer

prompt_templates.title: Grill my feature proposal
prompt_templates.prompt: Grill me on this plan: [describe your feature or plan]. Challenge my terminology against our existing domain model and help me update CONTEXT.md as we clarify concepts.

prompt_templates.title: Check my architecture decision
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to use this skill to document my architectural decision about [topic]. Help me understand if it's worth an ADR by questioning whether it's hard to reverse, surprising without context, and the result of real trade-offs.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have a significant architecture decision to document

prompt_templates.title: Find documentation gaps
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to cross-examine how [feature] works according to the code vs. how we describe it in our docs. Find any contradictions and update our CONTEXT.md to reflect the actual behavior.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you suspect docs are out of sync with implementation

prompt_templates.title: Multi-context session
prompt_templates.prompt: We have multiple bounded contexts. Use this skill to grill me on [topic], correctly identifying which context it belongs to and updating that context's CONTEXT.md.
prompt_templates.scenario: For monorepo or microservices with multiple domain contexts

output_examples.input: We're adding user accounts to handle billing
output_examples.output: Your CONTEXT.md distinguishes between 'User' (someone who logs in) and 'Customer' (someone who pays). Did you mean 'Customer' for billing? Also, your code shows Billing already references Customer records. Let's resolve this terminology before proceeding.

output_examples.input: I want to use Redis for caching
output_examples.output: This qualifies as an ADR candidate. Redis creates meaningful lock-in, someone reading the code will wonder why not Memcached, and you clearly evaluated alternatives. Let's document the specific reasons you chose Redis over the options you considered.

output_examples.input: How should we handle order cancellations?
output_examples.output: Your code shows orders can only be cancelled before fulfillment starts. Your CONTEXT.md mentions 'cancellation' but doesn't define its boundaries. Let's update: 'Cancellation' means voiding an order before shipment. After shipment, this becomes a 'return'. Does this match your intent?

best_practices: Run the grilling session before writing code, not after — catch terminology gaps early
Update CONTEXT.md in real-time as terms are resolved, not in a batch at the end
Use the three ADR criteria strictly — only create records for decisions that meet all conditions
anti_patterns: Batching CONTEXT.md updates until the session ends instead of capturing decisions as they happen
Offering ADRs for obvious or easily reversible decisions just to have documentation
Skipping the cross-reference step when existing code contradicts stated behavior

faq.question: What is the difference between this skill and a regular code review?
faq.answer: This skill focuses specifically on domain terminology and documented decisions, not code quality. It helps surface implicit knowledge before it becomes code, whereas code review happens after implementation.

faq.question: When should I create a CONTEXT.md vs an ADR?
faq.answer: CONTEXT.md captures domain vocabulary and relationships — ongoing terminology. ADRs document specific decisions that were hard to make and harder to reverse. Most decisions become part of CONTEXT.md; few rise to ADR level.

faq.question: Does this skill modify my code?
faq.answer: No. This skill only updates documentation files (CONTEXT.md and ADRs). It never writes or modifies application code.

faq.question: What makes a decision worthy of an ADR?
faq.answer: All three conditions must be true: the decision is hard to reverse, it would surprise a future reader without explanation, and it involved genuine alternatives with trade-offs you chose between.

faq.question: How does this skill handle multi-context repositories?
faq.answer: It reads CONTEXT-MAP.md to identify contexts, infers which context applies to the current topic, and updates the correct CONTEXT.md. If no map exists, it creates a single root CONTEXT.md.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for existing projects with poor documentation?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill intentionally starts fresh — creating CONTEXT.md lazily only when terms are first resolved. It builds documentation incrementally from the first grilling session forward.
